name: Bhaskara
dates_notes: Roughly contemporary with Śaṅkara; dated from internal references and citation chronology.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhāskara explicitly rejects Śaṅkara's māyā as a smuggled-in Buddhist concept; the polemic runs both directions through later commentary.

core_thesis:
Bhāskara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* organises Vedānta around one structural claim: difference (*bheda*) and non-difference (*abheda*) are both real features of the Brahman–world relation, but they hold under different conditions. *Abheda* is the underlying ontological truth; *bheda* arises through *upādhi*, the limiting adjunct — body, sense-organ, mental apparatus, the lived conditions of embodiment — that distinguishes the *jīva* from Brahman so long as embodiment persists. Drop the *upādhi* and the difference goes with it. The qualifier *aupādhika* in *aupādhika-bhedābheda* names this dependence on adjunct: difference is real, but it is conditional, not unconditional.

From this the rest follows. Brahman undergoes real transformation (*pariṇāma*) into the manifest world, so the world is *satya*; *jagat-satyatva* is non-negotiable. The individual self is non-different from Brahman in essence and really different in its embodied condition. Liberation is the dropping of adjunct, not the discovery that the difference was never there.

Bhāskara's polemical signature is the charge against Śaṅkara of *pracchanna-bauddha* — crypto-Buddhism — registered in the *Bhāṣya* itself. The argument is specific: *māyā-vāda* makes the world *mithyā*, *mithyā* is functionally indistinguishable from the *śūnyavādin*'s thesis that phenomena lack own-being, and a Vedāntin who arrives at this conclusion has imported the Buddhist position under a different name. The charge is structural, not abusive. It targets what Bhāskara takes to be the Advaitin's deviation from the *sūtras*' realist construal of *Brahma-pariṇāma*.

Soteriologically Bhāskara defends *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, the joint efficacy of knowledge and ritual action, against the Advaitin claim that knowledge alone suffices because the world it would act on is *mithyā*. His position is that the world's reality is presupposed by the very activity of knowing, so the dichotomy is misframed.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the earliest extant Bhedābhedin commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.
engaged_works.summary: Bhāskara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* — often called the *Bhāskarīya* — is the earliest extant *bhedābheda* commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's text and a direct rival to Śaṅkara's, very likely composed with Śaṅkara's reading already in view. Two argumentative tasks run through it. The first is constructive: each *adhikaraṇa* is read as licensing *aupādhika-bhedābheda*, with Brahman undergoing real *pariṇāma* into a real world and *bheda* arising through *upādhi* rather than appearance. The second is polemical: the *bhāṣya* mounts the *pracchanna-bauddha* charge against *māyā-vāda* — that an Advaitin who reduces the world to *mithyā* has covertly imported the *śūnyavādin*'s denial of own-being into Vedānta. Bhāskara also defends *jagat-satyatva*, the genuine plurality of embodied *jīvas*, and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* against the Advaitic insistence that knowledge alone suffices. Read against later *bhedābheda* developments, the *bhāṣya* preserves an early-medieval option in which neither Śaṅkara's *vivarta* nor the later Rāmānuja-line *śarīra-śarīrin* qualification governs the *sūtras*. The text is the foundational reference for any reconstruction of pre-Yādava Bhedābheda.

engaged_works.title: Gita-Bhashya fragments
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Surviving in fragmentary form; ascription accepted but the text is incomplete.
engaged_works.summary: A *Gītā-Bhāṣya* is ascribed to Bhāskara but survives only in fragmentary citation, principally in Madhusūdana and in several Advaita and Bhedābheda doxographies. Where the fragments are doctrinally legible, they extend the programme of the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. *Karma* and *jñāna* are read as jointly efficacious rather than as successive stages collapsing into pure *jñāna*. The *jīva* is taken as really, not illusorily, distinct from Brahman under embodied conditions. The *Gītā*'s theistic vocabulary is read at face value rather than as a concession to the unenlightened. Because the text is not preserved continuously, the responsible use is supplementary: as evidence for Bhāskara's reading-strategy on disputed verses (notably 2.12, 7.4–7, 13.2) rather than as an independent doctrinal source. The fragmentary state itself is historically informative; it marks how much of the pre-Rāmānuja Vaiṣṇava Vedānta record has been lost. [Direct citation of the surviving fragments NOT YET RETRIEVED in this entry.]
